Transcrição Completa: 2 Fit Tea CEO Lessons     [00:00]  "[applause] Thank you. Thank you. Thank you guys. Uh if you guys want to follow me, I'm the TCEO on Instagram. So, you know, check me out."   [00:12]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Fit Tea CEO - Michael"   NÚMEROS:  Started 2013, $10K initial investment   VISUAL:  Introdução do speaker e do case Fit Tea   [00:18]  "I did this little thing called Fit Te. Back in 2013, I was really obsessed with just like losing weight and finding an allnatural way to lose weight. So, a friend of mine was like, 'Oh, why don't you try detox teas?' So, I went and tried some detox tees."   [00:38]  "And the detox tea market at the time was really just yanky, really low-end teas that tasted really bad. And and I was the kind of person like, 'Oh man, this could be like a a thing I could ship. It was really light because I was shipping books on Amazon.'"   [00:58]  "And my my first education to entrepreneurship was just selling books on Amazon. So I was like, I want to make a product that's, you know, lightweight. So the first thing I tried tea, I was like, man, this is this is perfect."   [01:18]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Previous Experience Reference"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Comparação com experiência anterior (weed reference)   [01:22]  "And I had a a little bit of education in another industry. I was cutting it, I was bagging it, and I was selling it. And this was exactly the same thing, but legal. That's a that's a it's a weed reference if that went over your head."   [01:42]  "Um, so really fortunately, I was like, I'm going to go to Manufacturers for Tea and see if they could supply me. And at the time, I only had $10,000. I had saved up from hustling weed and little jobs here and there. So, this was a, you know, significant risk for me."   [02:08]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Early Instagram Marketing - 2013"   NÚMEROS:  $10 per post initial influencer payment, $10K first night sales   VISUAL:  Pioneer days de Instagram marketing   [02:15]  "And uh and Fitzy was, you know, really early for social media marketing. There was nobody nobody on Instagram marketing at all. And I was just like, I wonder if I, you know, contact people that are on the front page and offered them 10 bucks, would they post me? And sure enough, they're like, yeah, sure, why not?"   [02:38]  "Um, so I I took my 10K, I bagged up some tea, and and next thing you know, boom, I sold $10,000 in a night. And I was like, 'Holy fuck this is it. This is going to be way bigger than I thought.' Cuz my goal was to sell 14 bags, not, you know, 10 grand worth of, you know, tea that I had packed up at my mama's house."   [03:08]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Happy Tea - Public Company Exit"   NÚMEROS:  Sold to Better Moo (milk company)   VISUAL:  Segundo empreendimento mencionado   [03:15]  "Another company I had started was Happy Tea. Just going to mention it. I took that company public. That was another whole another story I'm not going to go into, but um took it public and I sold it to Better Moo. It was a a moo company that does uh like milk products and they acquired the company."   [03:38]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "The Beginning - Plastic Bags & Stickers"   NÚMEROS:  $2.50 all-in cost per bag, $45 retail price, ~94% gross margin   VISUAL:  Fotos dos primeiros produtos com embalagem básica   [03:45]  "Um but the start of fatigue like you can see the infies you know I labeled the bag just with a regular sticker and just you can see like the you know the beginnings of just me you know having this dream of like oh I can create a worldwide brand."   [04:08]  "I always used to tell people I want to be like Coca-Cola. I had this like obsession of like how can a guy that's creating a business get as much you know impressions as Coca-Cola company that's been around for 100 years"   [04:28]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "The Risk Philosophy"   NÚMEROS:  99% not taking enough risks   VISUAL:  Mensagem central sobre risk-taking   [04:35]  "and really in reality you need to take a significant amount of risk. I'm going to guess 99% of you in here are not taking enough risks to push your businesses forward. You're not willing because you have this innate feeling that you're going to live forever."   [04:58]  "I'm sorry to tell you, you're not. We are all destined to die. As as sucks as that message is, it's merely to remind you that that money that you're holding on like a little nugget and you look at your P&L and you're like, 'Oh my god, yes, I have $100,000.'"   [05:22]  "In reality, you're not really trying to grow to the next step because Fit grew because I was like, 'Fuck it. I'm going to put every single dollar and the confirmation of the business was constantly telling me to do so. Right? I was doing the work. I was making improvements and I would take the money and put it in."   [05:48]  "But to take something from $10,000, do you think it was because I was being careful? Absolutely not. Didn't give a fuck I was like, I'm going to put every dollar into marketing until this thing becomes Coca-Cola. And that was my obsession."   [06:12]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "First Production Line"   NÚMEROS:  14 bags/minute, took 1 year to build, outgrew in 3 months   VISUAL:  Vídeo da primeira máquina de produção   [06:18]  "So, like, you know, as as you can see, I started with just a plastic bag and sticker. The margins are absolutely ridiculous in in te especially if you're selling for $45. You know, one bag was probably all in $2.50. So, where I was getting my value obviously was marketing, right?"   [06:42]  "Here's me being cocky as fuck I thought I was I thought I was the man when I was recording this. This video is my first line machine. 14 bags a minute. I I thought I had the biggest dick in the world at this moment."   [07:05]  "I just thought I was just gonna take over the world with my 14 bags a minute machine that took almost a year to build. And literally within three months, we outgrew it. Three fuck months because I was all in. I was all in on spending the money into the business."   [07:32]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Peak Revenue"   NÚMEROS:  $1.3M/month, 150 employees   VISUAL:  Crescimento para massive scale   [07:38]  "We grew to 1.3 million. Huge business. And I had the obsession of how can I scale this business to be Coca-Cola. So obviously we were a dry product and eventually I took it to being an energy drink."   [07:58]  "Now the energy drink market is hard as fuck So I sold the company at this point but at at this point I had taken it to where I felt myself it it was going to go. I already had burned on all ends. I was all in made a ton of money"   [08:22]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Key Lessons"   NÚMEROS:  2 principais mensagens   VISUAL:  Core takeaways da apresentação   [08:28]  "and there's a certain period of time where if there's one thing you catch from me is number one you're not going to live forever. So really go for your business. Go for your dreams. put your money where your mouth is and and double your spend."   [08:52]  "Even like try for 30 days, double what you would normally do into your business, whether that's time, money, commitment, love, energy. I bet you it'll be a significant improvement. And what you really lose that one month of doubling the money that you would put into your business."   [09:18]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Find Your Rich"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Filosofia de definir o que é "rico" para você   [09:25]  "Um, the other aspect that I would want to give is find your rich. You know, when I was building this business, I thought, I need Lambos, I need Ferraris, I need mansions. But in reality, my rich is really low, cheap cigars, playing little games."   [09:48]  "Once you figure out you're rich, you're already rich. You really don't need to get the hundred million dollar companies that makes you lose all your hair and then come tell stories to people, you know?"   [10:10]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Challenges - Pirates"   NÚMEROS:  $350K ransom demand, millions in inventory, 2-3 months delay, 9 spelling errors, $2M+ affected   VISUAL:  História dos piratas e mercadoria sequestrada   [10:18]  "So, I'm going to talk about challenges. So challenges when you start to scale a business you get some fuck crazy things that happen. So one of them for me was pirates. So a lot of our stuffs was imported from other countries whether that was raw goods or materials."   [10:42]  "We had you know 150 people line manufacturing this. We were a pretty hoot and tootin business and we you know were getting a lot of stuff shipped to us. And one day I get a phone call. It's a bunch of digits and this Asian man. He's like hey I have your ballots."   [11:08]  "And I'm like, at the time we were getting pranked all the time from the staff. I had a lot of pranksters in my staff. So I thought it was my friend Jamal. I'm like, 'Well, fuck you, man. You know, hang up the phone.'"   [11:28]  "But in reality, I was talking to this this guy keeps calling and calling me. I'm like, 'Fuck, what is it?' He's like, 'We're going to burn your pallets if you don't give us 350,000.'"   [11:48]  "And I'm like, 'Well, fuck It's like millions of dollars worth of inventory, material goods, and be a significant loss if you would burn the the pallets. But I'm still fuck ridiculous. I'm like, 'Oh, fuck you, man. Burn my shit.' Still thinking is just Jamal fuck with me."   [12:12]  "And it no next day I got a call from the FBI and they're like, 'Oh, we've intercepted your pallets. Everything's okay.' It was probably not the next day, but" "and there's no problem here. And uh there's nothing to worry about. I'm like, 'All right, great. Thanks.'"   [12:38]  "You know, we get the pallets, it takes a long time, maybe two, three months. fuck comes off the thing. And then one of my staff's like, 'Hey, um sorry to tell you, but the the packaging has a bunch of spelling errors.'"   [12:58]  "And I'm like, 'What do you mean we got spelling?' Yeah, there's nine spelling errors. I'm like over $2 million worth of fuck I'm like, 'Fuck us.' The fuck pirates couldn't have burned these damn pallets, man. Sheesh."   [13:22]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Satan Attack - Black Friday 2014"   NÚMEROS:  $1M ad spend, 300K+ engaged followers attacking, 1,000 posts with 666 comments each   VISUAL:  Screenshots do ataque massivo no Instagram   [13:28]  "The next thing that happened to me was when we were growing, I was constantly doubling [laughter] and we got some man fuck hate when I think this was this was like 2014 and I said, 'You know what? I'm about to go crazy on Black Friday. I'm about to drop a million dollars worth of ads all at once."   [13:58]  "So, we basically spammed Instagram. We spammed the absolute fuck out of Instagram. And this guy named Satan of all fuck names decided to spam the fuck out of me. And he did not quit at all. His 300 and some thousand followers were engaged as fuck"   [14:25]  "We got death threats. I had people putting paint on my garage, people following me. It was fuck insane. It was absolute madness. You think you guys have had problems? You don't got fuck this kind of fuck crazy problems." You could not you could not do anything. You couldn't do fuck And uh so basically I had to hack Instagram."   [15:18]  "We basically hacked Instagram to basically do what Instagram does now where you can block people and block comments and filter. You know, we had like there were at the time I think there was like a thousand posts and every post had 666 on it. Like every fuck post like god damn a thousand posts. They went deep and they were attacking our ads." I'll tell your wife, fuck And that was it. That was no more Satan."   [16:18]  "[laughter] Hey, [applause] hey, I wanted to know where he wanted to know who was fuck I wanted to know everything. This guy's fuck my money."   [16:38]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "$100M Worth of Secrets"   NÚMEROS:  Referência ao Jay-Z quote   VISUAL:  Transição para lições práticas   [16:45]  "Okay, so like some some practicality. I don't want to just be, you know, stories. $100 million worth of secrets, you know, like Jay-Z says, I want to I want to give you million dollar, you know, lessons for $14.99, I think he says or something like that."   [17:08]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Rule #1: When You Give, You Get"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Filosofia central de negócios   [17:15]  "Uh, so my number one rule in life for business is when you give, you get. Your time, your money, your commitment, your energy, your love, your energy of your life. The more you give, the more you get."   [17:38]  "And uh a lot of times, you know, as business business people, we get kind of like lost in the shuffle of just the money, you know, the ones, the ones, the oneplus twos, but sometimes it's there's a human part to this."   [18:00]  "And sometimes you have to give back to where you're not going to gain absolutely fuck nothing. You're not going to gain at least on your P&L. But there's a snowball effect that happens that I don't know if it's just the universe, if you believe in God or you don't or whatever that energy, there's some type of energy that happens when you give."   [18:28]  "And the more that I break that rule or don't follow that rule, I don't get that same type of return that I'm hoping for. So when you give is my number one, by far my number one secret."   [18:48]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Give Away Product Strategy"   NÚMEROS:  Started 100/week → 1,000/week → 5,000/week   VISUAL:  Estratégia de product giveaway   [18:55]  "Um, and that applies, you know, directly to a business. You can give away your product. We did that for years. I was giving out thousands of bags of Fiti because Fiti was actually a really fuck great product."   [19:18]  "Like it was tasted great. People felt amazing when they drank it. They had energy from granada, different type of caffeinated feeling because it comes from a different alkaloid plant. You're typically used to just like you know caffeine. So a lot of people that drank fits was like damn I feel amazing."   [19:45]  "So we gave a lot of products in biosmosis by giving away the product. People would try it and then they would recommend it to somebody. So, we did that. Started at 100, then it was a thousand. fuck I think at the end we were like 5,000 a week or something like that. It was It was crazy."   [20:12]  "Again, like I said before, you got to have some big fuck to sell that much money worth of any fuck thing. Especially, we're talking tea, guys. Like, I don't think you're kind of like even comprehending how ridiculous it's hard how hard it is to sell that much product."   [20:38]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Celebrity Marketing - Kylie Jenner"   NÚMEROS:  $13K payment, $375K revenue that weekend   VISUAL:  Post da Kylie com Fit Tea   [20:45]  "Um, but we, you know, we worked with some of the biggest celebrities. Now obviously I'm on the spectrum of cuckoo for Cocoa Puff's craziness, but if you get it right, you can make a fuck ton of money."   [21:35]  "One of my favorite things about" "blah blah blah. She want she got paid and this is this is why I got paid. So drink this. Thank you."   [21:55]  "The Kylie post he said that was like one of the first ones we did with the Kardashians. I paid her $13,000 which at the time I thought was a fuck ton of money for one post. I was like, 'What in the fuck This fuck wants how much?' And she was like 16 years old."   [22:22]  "And uh paid that girl that money. And then that weekend we did like $375,000 all directly from that one singular post. And I was like, 'Holy fuck how many of these $13,000 post can we buy?' You know"   [22:48]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Call Customers to Say Thank You"   NÚMEROS:  50+ person customer service team no pico   VISUAL:  Filosofia de customer service   [22:55]  "at that point, it's something really simple. Call your customers just to say thank you. It's really just at the end of the day, business is people to people. We tend to forget that humans. We keep looking our analytics. We look at this. It's like humans, you know?"   [23:22]  "And uh at 50, we had phones everywhere because it was we were partially a social media company and partially a tea company, right? So no matter what, at any time if you didn't have nothing to fuck do, I'd be like, 'Yo, pick up a phone and start calling the customers. DM them, text them, talk to them, talk to them like a fuck human. Hey, how's it going? How's your health go?' Blah, blah, blah blah blah."   [23:55]  "It was always a part of our culture to be really a thank you. Thank you company."   [24:05]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Did You Bleed?"   NÚMEROS:  1 out of 5 people will die within 5 years   VISUAL:  Filosofia sobre taking risks e mortality   [24:12]  "Did you bleed? It's my favorite. Did you fuck bleed? Some of you just want to get rich or just want to make money, but you don't want to bleed. You ever seen, you know, Mike Tyson fight somebody and they lose and they're all fuck up at the end? Did they really lose? Did they really lose? They fought Mike Tyson."   [24:42]  "Imagine right now if I told you you go fight prime Mike Tyson. He just fuck your ass up. That would be such a story. I wish I could be like, 'Yeah, I fought Mike Tyson.' Like that would be so [laughter]"   [25:05]  "But that's the same way with business. Like a lot of times you got to like take your lickings and go for it. Like in the end of the day, you're dead. You're dead in the future. I'm sorry. I'm sorry. One of you will be gone in less than five years from now. Five. Five. Sorry. I'm sorry. It could be me."   [25:38]  "You know, when I was a kid, I was on a high bed with my mother and I rolled over and I fell over and I hit the ground and I hit my head. I got up and the first thing my mother says to me, she kept it so real. She was like, 'Life isn't fair and then you die.' I was four years old. I'm like the fuck [laughter] what?"   [26:08]  "It's just But I like to remind you because we get lost in the sauce of this, you know, this thing of like we're going to live forever or at least that mentality and being reminded that you're mortal reminds you that you can go bigger. You can go way bigger. You're taking it too safe. You can't get out alive."   [26:38]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "1% Rule"   NÚMEROS:  1% improvement daily = 100% eventually   VISUAL:  Compound improvement philosophy   [26:45]  "1% rule. 1% rule. You can make one 1% every single day. No matter what you can improve whatever that thing is, eventually it will become 100%. It's so simple in logic and but in reality a lot of times as entrepreneurs we want to do 10, we want to do 20, we want to do 30% of numbers, we want to do better, but 1% can add up really fast."   [27:22]  "And I'm guilty of that same mentality of like I want to do better. Let's build this thing and you know we can fuck do this and that whatever. But in reality it would hurt me because I was reaching too far in terms of like those 1% growth was better right?"   [27:48]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "1% Manufacturing Examples"   NÚMEROS:  $400K solution vs cellophane wrap   VISUAL:  Exemplos práticos de 1% improvements   [27:55]  "So like even like manufacturing was a huge challenge. I had no experience in manufacturing. Went to school for business management. Then all of a sudden I have this business that I had to manufacture fuck So like a lot of the times I would sit there with my stepdad at the time and we would be like how can we make you know 1% improvement to the line?"   [28:28]  "And it'd be such stupid fuck fuck that you wouldn't even like you think and go, 'Oh my god, that's the thing that we did that made it better.' Well, a perfect example, tea gets on a conveyor belt, goes up a ladder, goes all the way up to a a weighing scale at the top."   [28:52]  "We have this fuck stupid problem where the tea bags would fall. They would fall off the thing and we'd have people come give us estimate. They're like, 'Yeah, that's going to cost uh, you know, 400,000 to fix that problem, Mike.' And then one day me and my dad are looking at each other like, 'What do you think about just putting some cellophane up there? Just wrap the whole entire thing.' I'm like, 'Yeah, it probably would work.' And then we cellophane the whole thing."   [29:35]  "And simple solution 1% 1% little things that you find simple thing. We had a staff that had to pick up boxes off the ground. Constantly was causing a problem and delay of picking up the boxes. Oh, why don't we get a cart? So simple. Little stupid things. You know, you don't you don't know will cause like a significant amount of money."   [30:08]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Protect Your Mind - Dr. Claire Weekes"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Livro "Hope and Help for Your Nerves"   [30:15]  "Protect your mind. Dr. Claire Weeks, I'm about to solve your anxieties for the rest of your life. If you have any issues with anxieties and if you are true entrepreneurs trying to make big money, then you have problems with this all the time. Anxiety, panic attacks, fear."   [30:42]  "Dr. Claire Weekes wrote a book called Hope and Help for Your Nerves. I went through a disorder. When I got to my peaks and I thought I could do everything and I was going to be the next Steve Jobs, life decided to go pow and give me a disorder. And I had full GAD, so general anxiety disorder."   [31:12]  "And um most people when you get that, they you go to a clinic, go to a doctor, like, hey, here's some pills and get the fuck out, right? There's no real solution. Dr. Cla Weekes talks about her theory. It's called the accepted method."   [31:35]  "The accepted method allows you to change your perspective on anxiety. Instead of it being a thing where you're going, I want it to go away. I hate how I feel. It becomes a thing that I accept this. I'm going to allow it. And that little change can make a significant difference."   [31:58]  "So, if you're struggling with this, look up her book. It's called Hope and Help for Your Nerves. Amazing book and it's and it's an amazing skill to have for the rest of your life."   [32:18]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Stop Selling, Start Serving"   NÚMEROS:  50+ person customer service team   VISUAL:  Customer service philosophy   [32:25]  "Um, [clears throat] stop selling, start serving. You know, again, we had a culture of just really working really hard at calling our customers constantly, you know, trying to get feedback, trying to be better, trying to give if even like we had even remotely if a person wanted wanted a refund, it would be an overload of fuck that we would do to make sure that they felt heard"   [32:58]  "make sure that we were like, 'Oh, you need another bag?' You know, we wanted to create lifelong customers that were willing to pay us again and again and again. So sometimes you got to take the ser you know the selling part out of the business and go more towards the customer service and serving part."   [33:25]  "So like the peaks of fit to you, I had a whole 50 plus customer service team. It was uh very significant part of our growth was constantly not forgetting that it's humans that you're selling." I sent her the money and I was going to Arkansas. We already had gotten the photos before I got to Arkansas and we pitched Walmart and we got the stupid deal because of that that one photo." [36:38]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Additional Tips"   NÚMEROS:  Múltiplas dicas rápidas   VISUAL:  Lista de tactical tips   [36:45]  "Hire fast, fire fast. these type of things I'm just going to go quick over. You know, a lot of times we overthink things. The hiring process, you get kind of crazy. You interview that. Just hire them and then fire them quickly."   [37:05]  "Like it's really just a in-n-out get the human aspect out on the management part. I know it's hard because you get to know somebody, you're like, 'Oh man, I like this guy. He plays Smash.' But you got to like you got to go quick with it because then you find the really good people, you know, fast."   [37:28]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Follow Trends - Catch Me Outside Girl"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Viral moment capitalizado   [37:35]  "Follow trends. Um" "we were the one that did the catch me outside girl. She was like literally just had trended the week before and I was like, 'Oh, we got to get her.' And so we paid her to say, 'Catch me outside with it.'"   [38:00]  "But then something happened over time. I I was so big and so so worldrenown as far as like the culture. It started becoming synonymous with like just organic" "people."   [38:18]  "I was at a party when that happened. I was like, 'What the fuck did she just say fit?' Because at first I was not 100% sure, but yeah, she said fits. And um so it became like generic like you know band-aid or you know these type of things kind of happen."   [38:45]  (Fim da transcrição no timestamp exato)     📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO   NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS: Started 2013 com $10K $10 pagamento inicial por post de influencer $10K vendido primeira noite $2.50 custo all-in por bag $45 preço de venda (~94% gross margin) 14 bags/minuto primeira máquina (1 ano build, outgrew em 3 meses) $1.3M/month no pico 150 employees no pico $13K Kylie Jenner post → $375K revenue weekend $2.7M Kim Kardashian payment $350K ransom pirates demand $2M+ inventory affected por pirates 9 spelling errors no packaging $1M Black Friday ad spend (2014) 300K+ Satan followers attacking 1,000 posts attacked com 666 comments cada 100 → 1,000 → 5,000 bags/week giveaway 50+ person customer service team 99% not taking enough risks 1% daily improvement = 100% eventually $400K quote vs cellophane wrap solution 1 em 5 pessoas morrerá em 5 anos   ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:   Early Instagram Marketing (2013): Pioneer em Instagram marketing $10 per post para front page influencers Ninguém estava fazendo marketing no Instagram First mover advantage massivo   Product Strategy: Lightweight product (easy shipping) Natural detox tea (better than competition) Taste + energy from different alkaloid (guarana) Started bagging at mom's house Basic packaging: plastic bag + sticker Ridiculous margins (~94%)   Risk Philosophy: "You're going to die" - core message Put every dollar back into marketing Double your spend for 30 days experiment All-in mentality from day one Obsession: become Coca-Cola Not being careful = growth   "When You Give, You Get" - Rule #1: Give away product strategy Started 100/week → 5,000/week giveaways Build through word of mouth Snowball effect from universe/energy Give where you won't gain on P&L Most important secret   Celebrity/Influencer Marketing: Kylie Jenner: $13K → $375K weekend Kim Kardashian: $2.7M → Walmart deal DJ Khaled partnerships "Catch Me Outside" girl trend capitalization Millions spent on celebrity posts Convicted in brand building Calculate: how many $13K posts can we buy?   Customer Service Philosophy: "Stop selling, start serving" Call customers just to say thank you 50+ person CS team at peak Phones everywhere in office DM, text, talk like humans Overload of service for refunds Create lifelong customers "Thank you company" culture   Manufacturing 1% Rule: 1% daily improvement = 100% eventually Don't reach for 10-30% jumps Stupid simple solutions work Example: $400K quote vs cellophane wrap Example: Cart instead of picking boxes off ground Stepdad partnership on improvements No manufacturing experience initially   Scaling Challenges:   Pirates: Asian man called demanding $350K ransom Thought it was prank (Jamal) FBI intercepted 2-3 months delay 9 spelling errors in $2M+ packaging Wished pirates had burned it   Satan Attack (Black Friday 2014): $1M ad spend dropped Spammed Instagram heavily User "Satan" with 300K+ followers attacked Death threats, paint on garage, stalking 1,000 posts with 666 comments each Couldn't block comments (pre-feature) Had to hack Instagram Solution: Hired PI, found mistress, blackmail   Mental Health Protection: Dr. Claire Weekes - "Hope and Help for Your Nerves" Acceptance Method for anxiety GAD (General Anxiety Disorder) experience Change perspective: accept vs fight Pills aren't the solution Lifelong skill for entrepreneurs   Find Your Rich: Thought needed Lambos, Ferraris, mansions Reality: cheap cigars, little games Once you know your rich, you're already rich Don't need $100M company that makes you lose hair Low cost of personal happiness   Did You Bleed?: Mike Tyson fight analogy Taking lickings and going for it Story value even in "losing" Mortality reminder: can't get out alive Take bigger risks than you think 1 in 5 will die within 5 years   Operational Tips: Hire fast, fire fast Don't overthink hiring process Remove human attachment quickly Follow trends immediately Became generic like "Band-Aid" Blog posts from info products Drive traffic to content DM and email obsession Data obsession ("data fuck") Buy email lists constantly Mathematics = universal truth   Happy Tea Exit: Took public Sold to Better Moo (milk company) Different story not covered in detail   Energy Drink Pivot: Moved from dry tea to energy drink Energy drink market "hard as fuck" Sold company at this point Took it as far as felt possible   Consulting Offer: Not typical consultant "Not patty cake" - tells it like it is Changes lives with single tap Brandon Carter example: Music7 → King Keo TikTok early adoption push Available for life-changing consulting   DADOS FINANCEIROS:   Initial Investment & Returns: $10K saved from hustling $10K sold first night Goal was 14 bags, not $10K ROI immediate and massive   Margins: $2.50 all-in cost per bag $45 retail price ~94% gross margin Value in marketing, not product   Peak Performance: $1.3M/month revenue 150 employees Line manufacturing at scale Multiple production lines   Celebrity ROI: Kylie: $13K → $375K weekend (2,785% ROI) Kim: $2.7M → Walmart deal (immeasurable) DJ Khaled partnerships Millions total in influencer spend   Crisis Costs: $350K pirate ransom demand $2M+ inventory affected $1M Black Friday ad spend (2014) $400K manufacturing solution quote (avoided with cellophane)   Customer Acquisition: Started $10 per influencer post Scaled to millions for top celebrities Product giveaway: 100 → 5,000 bags/week 50+ person CS team overhead   Exit Details: Sold Fit Tea (amount not disclosed) Happy Tea sold to Better Moo Took Happy Tea public first Board involvement in public company   DURAÇÃO:  38:45  FORMATO:  Apresentação presencial em mastermind com Q&A
